cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The SI unit representing $6.022 \times 10^{23}$ particles of a substance

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The mass in grams of one mole of a substance; numerically equal to the atomic or molecular mass in amu

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Atoms of the same element that have different numbers of neutrons and therefore different mass numbers

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms of each element in a compound

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A notation showing how electrons are distributed among subshells in an atom, e.g., $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The net positive charge experienced by a valence electron after accounting for the shielding effect of inner electrons

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The energy required to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom in its ground state

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: An atom's ability to attract electrons toward itself when participating in a chemical bond

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Electrons in the outermost occupied shell of an atom; these electrons participate in chemical bonding

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Electrons in inner shells of an atom that reduce the effective nuclear charge experienced by valence electrons

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The principle that electrons occupy the lowest available energy subshell before filling higher-energy subshells

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The reduction in effective nuclear charge on valence electrons caused by inner-shell electrons partially blocking the nuclear attraction

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The spectroscopic technique that bombards a sample with high-energy X-rays, measures the energies of ejected electrons to determine orbital binding energies

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A spectrum showing peaks at different binding energies corresponding to electrons in different subshells; peak height indicates relative number of electrons

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Determined that atoms have a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus by firing alpha particles at gold foil and observing back-scattering

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Proposed that electrons orbit the nucleus only in quantized energy levels and that light is emitted when electrons transition between levels

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Described the electrostatic law of attraction and repulsion between charged particles — the force is proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of distance

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: His constant $6.022 \times 10^{23}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$ defines the number of particles in one mole

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Arranged elements by increasing atomic mass, revealing the periodic recurrence of chemical properties now illustrated in the periodic table

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Proposed that energy is emitted or absorbed in discrete packets called quanta; $E = h\nu$

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Demonstrated the photoelectric effect — showing light behaves as packets of energy (photons) — laying a foundation for quantum mechanics

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which element among Period 2 elements has the highest first ionization energy?

choices.text: Lithium (Li)
choices.explanation: Lithium has the lowest first ionization energy in Period 2 because it has only one valence electron and the lowest effective nuclear charge in the period.

choices.text: Carbon (C)
choices.explanation: Carbon has moderate ionization energy for Period 2 but is not the highest; elements farther right with more protons and similar shielding have higher values.

choices.text: Neon (Ne)
choices.explanation: Neon has the highest first ionization energy in Period 2 because it has the greatest nuclear charge (10 protons) with the same shielding as other Period 2 elements, giving the highest effective nuclear charge.

choices.text: Fluorine (F)
choices.explanation: Fluorine has a very high ionization energy but it is slightly lower than neon's because neon has one more proton with similar shielding.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Atomic radius decreases across Period 2 from left (Li) to right (Ne). Which explanation best accounts for this trend?

choices.text: The number of electron shells increases across the period
choices.explanation: All Period 2 elements have 2 electron shells. The number of shells stays constant across a period; adding shells occurs as you go down a group.

choices.text: The nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, pulling electrons closer
choices.explanation: Across Period 2, each element gains one proton but valence electrons are added to the same shell with similar shielding. The increasing nuclear charge exerts more attractive force, drawing the electron cloud inward.

choices.text: Electrons repel each other more strongly in heavier elements
choices.explanation: Electron-electron repulsion slightly counteracts nuclear attraction but is not the primary driver of the decreasing radius trend. Nuclear charge increase dominates.

choices.text: Heavier atoms have denser nuclei that compress the electron cloud
choices.explanation: Nuclear density does not directly compress electron clouds. The electrostatic attraction between nucleus and electrons (Coulomb's law) is what determines atomic radius.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A mass spectrum of an element shows two peaks: one at $m/z = 10$ with 20% relative abundance and one at $m/z = 11$ with 80% relative abundance. What does this indicate?

choices.text: The element exists as two different compounds with molecular masses 10 and 11
choices.explanation: Mass spectra of pure elements show isotopes, not compounds. The two peaks represent atoms with the same proton count but different neutron counts (isotopes).

choices.text: The element has two naturally occurring isotopes; the one with mass 11 is more abundant
choices.explanation: Each peak in a mass spectrum represents a different isotope. The peak at m/z = 11 has higher relative abundance (80%), so that isotope is more common in nature.

choices.text: The element gained and lost a proton to form ions of different masses
choices.explanation: Isotopes differ in neutron count, not proton count. Changing the proton count changes the element identity entirely.

choices.text: The two peaks represent the same isotope measured at different ionization energies
choices.explanation: Each distinct m/z value in a mass spectrum represents a species with a different mass-to-charge ratio, indicating a different isotope — not the same isotope measured differently.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: An element has two isotopes: isotope A with mass 10 u and 20% natural abundance, and isotope B with mass 11 u and 80% natural abundance. What is the average atomic mass?

choices.explanation: 10.5 would be the simple average of 10 and 11, ignoring abundance. Average atomic mass must be weighted by fractional abundance.
This value incorrectly weights isotope A too heavily. Isotope B (mass 11) has 80% abundance and should contribute more to the average.
Weighted average = $(0.20)(10) + (0.80)(11) = 2.0 + 8.8 = 10.8\ \text{u}$. The higher abundance of isotope B pulls the average closer to 11.
11.0 would be correct only if isotope B had 100% abundance. Since 20% of atoms have mass 10, the average is pulled below 11.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A PES spectrum of an element shows three peaks at binding energies of approximately 1000 eV, 50 eV, and 10 eV. What does each peak represent?

choices.text: The three peaks represent three different elements in the sample
choices.explanation: PES measures electrons from a single element. Different peaks represent electrons in different subshells of the same element, not different elements.

choices.text: Each peak represents electrons in a different orbital subshell; higher binding energy = electrons held more tightly (closer to nucleus)
choices.explanation: In PES, the x-axis shows binding energy (energy needed to remove each electron). Core electrons (closest to nucleus, highest nuclear attraction) have the highest binding energies. Valence electrons have the lowest.

choices.text: The three peaks represent electrons with different speeds after ejection
choices.explanation: PES plots binding energy vs. relative number of electrons, not electron speed. The binding energy is determined by the subshell, not by measurement conditions.

choices.text: Each peak represents a different isotope of the element contributing different electron energies
choices.explanation: Isotopes have the same electron configuration and nearly identical binding energies for their electrons. PES peaks correspond to electron subshells, not isotopes.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Why does fluorine (Z=9) have a higher first ionization energy than oxygen (Z=8), despite both being in Period 2?

choices.text: Fluorine has fewer valence electrons than oxygen
choices.explanation: Fluorine has 7 valence electrons; oxygen has 6. Fluorine has more valence electrons, not fewer.

choices.text: Fluorine has a higher nuclear charge with similar shielding, giving a greater effective nuclear charge that holds electrons more tightly
choices.explanation: Both F and O have electrons in the second shell with similar shielding from core electrons. F has one more proton (Z=9 vs 8), increasing effective nuclear charge and making it harder to remove a valence electron.

choices.text: Oxygen has a paired electron in a 2p orbital that experiences extra repulsion, making it easier to remove
choices.explanation: This explains why oxygen's ionization energy is slightly lower than nitrogen's (oxygen has a paired 2p electron). But it does not explain why fluorine is higher than oxygen; that is due to nuclear charge.

choices.text: Fluorine's electrons are in higher-energy orbitals than oxygen's electrons
choices.explanation: Both F and O have valence electrons in 2p orbitals — the same energy level. Higher-energy orbitals would actually make electrons easier to remove, not harder.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: The second ionization energy of sodium (Na) is approximately 10 times greater than its first ionization energy. What best explains this dramatic jump?

choices.text: The second electron removed comes from a different element than the first
choices.explanation: Both ionization energies refer to removing electrons from the same sodium atom (just sequentially). There is no change in element identity.

choices.text: After removing the first valence electron, the second electron must be removed from a core shell much closer to the nucleus and more strongly held
choices.explanation: Sodium's first electron is a lone valence electron (3s) easily removed. The second electron comes from the 2p subshell — a core electron much closer to the nucleus with far less shielding, requiring dramatically more energy to remove.

choices.text: The atom becomes negatively charged after the first ionization, making the second removal harder
choices.explanation: After losing one electron, Na becomes Na⁺ (positively charged), not negative. A cation holds its remaining electrons more tightly than the neutral atom, but the jump is specifically explained by moving to a core shell.

choices.text: Sodium forms an unusually stable electronic configuration after the first ionization
choices.explanation: While Na⁺ has a noble gas configuration, the key reason for the jump is quantitative — the second electron is in a core shell (n=2) much closer to 11 protons with far less shielding than the n=3 valence electron.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which of the following best explains the trend in electronegativity across Period 3 from Na to Cl?

choices.text: The number of electron shells increases from Na to Cl
choices.explanation: All Period 3 elements have electrons in shells 1, 2, and 3. The number of electron shells is the same across an entire period.

choices.text: The nuclear charge increases while shielding remains approximately constant, increasing the pull on bonding electrons
choices.explanation: Across Period 3, the number of protons increases from 11 (Na) to 17 (Cl) while the shielding from core electrons stays similar. This raises effective nuclear charge, making atoms pull electron pairs in bonds more strongly.

choices.text: The atomic radius increases across Period 3, spreading electrons farther and reducing electronegativity
choices.explanation: Atomic radius actually decreases across a period. Electronegativity increases as radius decreases because the nucleus attracts bonding electrons from a shorter distance.

choices.text: Elements in Period 3 gain more electrons from left to right, causing greater electron-electron repulsion
choices.explanation: More electrons do not directly increase electronegativity. Electronegativity is determined by how strongly the nucleus can attract shared electrons in a bond, which depends on effective nuclear charge.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A student determines an empirical formula of $CH_2O$ for an unknown compound. The molar mass of the compound is determined to be approximately 180 g/mol. What is the molecular formula?

choices.text: $CH_2O$
choices.explanation: The empirical formula $CH_2O$ has molar mass 30 g/mol. Since the actual molar mass is 180 g/mol and 180/30 = 6, the molecular formula must be six times the empirical formula.
$C_3H_6O_3$ has molar mass 90 g/mol (3×12 + 6×1 + 3×16 = 90). This is only three times the empirical formula, not six times.
Molar mass of $CH_2O$ = 12 + 2 + 16 = 30 g/mol. Ratio = 180/30 = 6. Molecular formula = $(CH_2O)_6 = C_6H_{12}O_6$ (glucose). This matches 6×12 + 12×1 + 6×16 = 180 g/mol.
$C_4H_8O_4$ has molar mass 120 g/mol (4×12 + 8 + 4×16 = 120), which does not match the 180 g/mol molar mass.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: An element forms a 2+ cation. Which of the following is consistent with the electron configuration of this element?

choices.text: The neutral atom has configuration $[Ar]3d^{10}4s^2$
choices.explanation: Zinc ([Ar]3d¹⁰4s²) loses the two 4s electrons to form Zn²⁺ with configuration [Ar]3d¹⁰. This is consistent with a stable 2+ cation.

choices.text: The neutral atom has configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^1$
choices.explanation: This is sodium, which has one valence electron and typically forms a 1+ cation, not 2+.

choices.text: The neutral atom has configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^5$
choices.explanation: This is fluorine, which has 7 valence electrons and typically gains one electron to form F⁻, not loses two to form 2+.

choices.text: The neutral atom has configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^3$
choices.explanation: This is phosphorus, which has 5 valence electrons. It commonly forms P³⁻ (gaining 3 electrons) or shares electrons in covalent bonds — it does not typically form a 2+ cation.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Adding a neutron to a neutral atom changes which of the following?

choices.text: The atomic number only
choices.explanation: Atomic number = number of protons. Adding a neutron does not change the proton count, so atomic number remains the same.

choices.text: The mass number only
choices.explanation: Mass number = protons + neutrons. Adding a neutron increases mass number by 1. The atomic number (proton count) and chemical behavior are unchanged — the result is a different isotope of the same element.

choices.text: Both the atomic number and the mass number
choices.explanation: The atomic number is determined solely by proton count, which does not change when a neutron is added. Only mass number changes.

choices.text: The chemical properties of the atom
choices.explanation: Chemical behavior is determined by electron configuration, which is based on atomic number (proton count). Isotopes of the same element have essentially identical chemical properties.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A student correctly writes the electron configuration of an element as $[Ne]3s^2 3p^4$. Which element is this, and how many valence electrons does it have?

choices.text: Sulfur; 6 valence electrons
choices.explanation: The [Ne] core accounts for 10 electrons. Adding $3s^2 3p^4$ gives 6 more = 16 total electrons = sulfur (Z=16). The valence electrons are those in n=3: 2+4 = 6.

choices.text: Oxygen; 6 valence electrons
choices.explanation: Oxygen (Z=8) has configuration $1s^2 2s^2 2p^4$, not $[Ne]3s^2 3p^4$. Oxygen is in Period 2 (n=2 valence shell), not Period 3.

choices.text: Chlorine; 7 valence electrons
choices.explanation: Chlorine has configuration $[Ne]3s^2 3p^5$ (one additional electron in 3p). The given configuration $3p^4$ does not match chlorine.

choices.text: Phosphorus; 5 valence electrons
choices.explanation: Phosphorus has configuration $[Ne]3s^2 3p^3$ (three electrons in 3p). The given configuration shows $3p^4$, which is one more electron than phosphorus.

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: Which of the following best describes the relationship between nuclear charge and atomic radius within a group of the periodic table (top to bottom)?

choices.text: Both nuclear charge and atomic radius increase going down a group
choices.explanation: Going down a group, each element has more protons (higher nuclear charge) AND more electron shells. The addition of electron shells outweighs the effect of increased nuclear charge, resulting in a larger atomic radius despite more protons.

choices.text: Nuclear charge increases while atomic radius decreases going down a group
choices.explanation: This describes the trend going across a period, not down a group. Going down a group, each element adds a new electron shell, causing atomic radius to increase despite higher nuclear charge.

choices.text: Both nuclear charge and atomic radius decrease going down a group
choices.explanation: Nuclear charge always increases when you move to higher-Z elements. Going down a group, atomic number increases, so nuclear charge increases — not decreases.

choices.text: Nuclear charge stays the same going down a group because all elements in a group have the same number of valence electrons
choices.explanation: Nuclear charge (atomic number) increases going down a group, even though valence electron count stays constant. For example, Group 1 elements go from Li (Z=3) to Na (Z=11) to K (Z=19).

cards.subject: ap-chemistry
cards.prompt: A student determines the percent composition by mass of a compound: 40.0% C, 6.7% H, and 53.3% O. What is the empirical formula?

choices.text: $CH_2O$
choices.explanation: Convert to moles: C: 40.0/12.0 = 3.33 mol; H: 6.7/1.0 = 6.7 mol; O: 53.3/16.0 = 3.33 mol. Divide by smallest (3.33): C:1, H:2, O:1 → empirical formula $CH_2O$.

choices.text: $CHO$
choices.explanation: After converting to moles and dividing, H has a ratio of 2 relative to C and O, not 1. The H:C ratio is approximately 2:1.
$C_2H_4O_2$ has the same ratio as $CH_2O$ but is not the simplest whole-number ratio. The empirical formula must be the simplest ratio, which is $CH_2O$.

choices.text: $CH_3O$
choices.explanation: $CH_3O$ would require the mole ratio of H to C to be 3:1. The calculation gives H:C = 6.7:3.33 ≈ 2:1, not 3:1.
